title: Springer Mountain
body: Southern terminus of the AT since 1958 (it was originally Mount Oglethorpe, but development pushed it north). The bronze plaque at the summit reads 'A footpath for those who seek fellowship with the wilderness.'

title: Neel Gap — Mountain Crossings
body: The only place the AT passes through a building. The breezeway of this 1937 stone outfitter is famous for hikers shedding gear after Blood Mountain — the trees outside are draped with discarded boots from thru-hikers learning what they really need.

title: Nantahala Outdoor Center (Wesser)
body: The trail crosses the Nantahala River on a footbridge here, dropping to one of its lowest points in the south at 1,723 ft. The NOC has been a hiker resupply hub since the 1970s and is famous for its cheeseburgers — and the brutal climb out either direction.

title: Hot Springs, NC
body: The trail walks straight down Bridge Street through this tiny mountain town, blazes painted right on the sidewalks. Named for the natural hot springs along the French Broad River; the AT was rerouted into town in 1987 specifically because so many hikers were detouring in.

title: Damascus, VA — Trail Days
body: 'The friendliest town on the trail.' Hosts Trail Days every May, the largest gathering of AT hikers anywhere — past and present hikers descend on Damascus for a weekend of tents, gear vendors, and a hiker parade. The trail uses local sidewalks for about a mile through town.

title: McAfee Knob
body: The most photographed spot on the entire AT — a flat sandstone ledge that juts into thin air at 3,197 ft. About 4 miles round-trip from the VA-311 trailhead, making it doable as a day hike and creating perpetual photo lines on summer weekends.

title: Harpers Ferry, WV
body: Headquarters of the Appalachian Trail Conservancy and the AT's traditional 'psychological midpoint' (though it's actually about 3 miles south of the true halfway). Every thru-hiker stops at the ATC office to get their photo taken and added to the year's hiker register.

title: Bear Mountain Bridge
body: The trail crosses the Hudson River on this 1924 suspension bridge — and within Bear Mountain State Park reaches its lowest point on the entire AT at 124 ft above sea level, beside the bear cages of the Trailside Zoo.

title: Mt. Washington — Lakes of the Clouds
body: At 6,288 ft, the highest peak in the northeast and home to 'some of the worst weather in the world' — a hurricane-force gust is recorded here on average every third day. The Lakes of the Clouds Hut just below the summit can put up hikers for the night with a meal.

title: Mahoosuc Notch
body: Widely called the hardest mile of the entire AT — a glacier-carved cleft choked with car-sized boulders. Most hikers spend over an hour scrambling, crawling under rocks, and tossing packs ahead. Year-round ice persists in the deeper crevices.

title: Mt. Katahdin — Baxter Peak
body: Northern terminus, 5,267 ft. Within Baxter State Park, which closes to AT hikers October 15 — the source of the thru-hiker scramble each fall. The wooden 'KATAHDIN' summit sign is the most-photographed object on the AT after McAfee Knob.

title: Roan Highlands — Carvers Gap
body: A long stretch of open balds — grassy mountaintops with 360° views, rare on the otherwise tree-tunneled AT. Round Bald, Jane Bald, and Grassy Ridge in succession make this one of the most photographed traverses south of New England.
